title: Tiger Woods Arrested On DUI Charge: Masters Plans In Jeopardy
excerpt: Tiger Woods was arrested on suspicion of driving under the influence following a car crash on Jupiter Island, Florida. With the Masters just weeks away, his competitive future remains uncertain.
body:
The Latest Chapter in a Troubling Pattern

Tiger Woods, 50, was arrested Friday afternoon after rolling his Land Rover on Beach Road in Jupiter Island, Florida, following what authorities say was an attempt to overtake a pressure cleaner truck at high speed. He has been charged with driving under the influence, property damage, and refusal to submit to a urine test, though he passed a breathalyser test with "triple zeros," according to Martin County Sheriff John Budensiek.

Woods, who is 82nd on the PGA Tour all-time wins list alongside Sam Snead, was released on bail after eight hours in custody—the minimum allowed under Florida law. No one was injured in the incident.

Woods Age

Charges Filed
3
Masters Majors
5
Days to Masters
11

Not Alcohol—But What?

Budensiek emphasised that investigators were "really not suspicious of alcohol being involved." Crash scene DUI investigators noted that Woods "did exemplify signs of impairment" during roadside tests, and Woods explained this partly by referencing the "injuries and surgeries that he's had." The sheriff left the cause of impairment unclear: "We will never get definitive results as to what he was impaired on at the time of the crash."

A Conversation Woods May Have Wanted to Avoid

Woods was described as "co-operative but was trying not to incriminate himself," and his refusal of a urine test is a decision protected by law but has left investigators without conclusive toxicology evidence. This echoes his 2017 arrest, when police found him slumped at the wheel of a parked Mercedes near his home; that case revealed multiple legal medications and marijuana in his system, leading to a guilty plea to reckless driving and one year's probation.

The Masters Question

Woods has not ruled out competing in next month's Masters—an event where he has triumphed five times, most recently in 2019. But the landscape has shifted dramatically. He returned to competition just this week at the TGL Finals after recovering from an Achilles tendon rupture and back surgery. His appearance Friday was his first competitive golf in over a year. Now, with misdemeanour charges pending and his medical status in question, a Masters appearance looks increasingly unlikely. The Augusta National story Woods wanted to write this spring—comeback against the odds—now hangs in the balance.
